I usually wouldn't get so excited over something as seemingly mundane a hand held stapler, but I used to have a really solid-feeling one (brand unknown) that reliably stapled sheets with a crisp snap, and an easy push would ALWAYS create a tight bond with no jams. And NO need to "push it just right." But I lost it 15 years ago, and since then keep trying the "feature laden," shoddy-feeling pieces of crap from the big-name office stores. It's all I could find. Even when I would get one that seemed to be working maybe 75% as well as my trusted lost hand held (though with a less-solid feel), within six months I'd be struggling with "bonds" where the staple didn't fold, repeated jams, and then springs would start falling out -- crap, crap, crap crap.

When I recently finally threw up my hands at the nearly-worthless piece of junk my latest (from OfficeMax as I recall) had become, I started searching. Then I saw this Skilcraft, which is a bit more expensive and FAR, FAR harder to locate than the "big name" junk. Seems that most of the few sites that carry it don't even bother to give the brand name (you can verify by the part number instead). top_profane.title: BRAVO!!  There IS sill a NON-JUNK hand held stapler!!!

top_profane.text: This printer sucks. Sure the printer is inexpensive--and prints great pictures...when it works. Problem is that, without warning, it will stop recognizing ink cartridges (even though they're new GENUINE Epson cartridges!). ALSO the printer nozzles keep clogging. You'll be printing along happily and BAM! black will clog up and stop printing. So, you'll give the printer nozzles a cleaning. Once that's done you'll run the nozzle check to see if everything is hunky dory only to find (SURPRISE!) that not only does black STILL not print, but neither does photo-black! WTF? So, you run the nozzle cleaner again, then print the nozzle check AND NOW YELLOW HAS ALSO STOPPED PRINTING!!!!!.... And if you repeat the previous steps? It's likely that either Cyan or Magenta will have joined Black, Yellow, and Photoblack in the non-printing arena.  NOW, if you're one of the lucky ones and, after multiple nozzle cleanings, the printer suddenly begins printing perfectly again, you're going to find out that ALL of your PREVIOUSLY FULL ink cartridges are now NEARLY EMPTY!!!!!........WHERE DOES ALL THE INK GO???? So now you have to go off and buy some more CRIMINALLY EXPENSIVE Epson ink only to have the cycle repeat itself in another month or two! Thank you, Epson! top_profane.text:
I would have rated it a 2 or 3 as a so-so, mediocre product but for the sheer fabrication in the use of the term "heavy duty" to describe this sharpener. It's not, It's made of very lightweight plastic, weighing about three ounces. Feels as if it would break if it fell onto a carpeted floor. The automatic shutoff mechanism is crap crap crap crap crap, unless your objective is to stop sharpening before the point gets sharp. I bought two cheaper Bostich sharpeners at the same time. Both are much better. I also have a Royal "The Point," which is so old that I don't remember when I got it. It is a lot better, too, after all these years, and it runs on AA batteries.

This sharpener is not junk. It does sharpen eventually if you stick the pencil in three times after the first two automatic shutoffs.

Probably, automatic shutoff is per se a bad idea. Anyone but a complete moron can tell with my other sharpeners from the sound and feel that the pencil has been fully sharpened. I don't get the point of automatically shutting it off. My other ones automatically shut off when I pull the pencil out, duh.
top_profane.title: Not Heavy Duty; Shutoff Sucks; Too Light To Be A Paperweight

top_profane.text: So, I'm doing the NY Times crossword puzzle with my Ticonderoga #2 pencil and the clue that I was on was 13(9).  That's it, 13(9).  I had no idea what the Hell this was.  What is it?  The symbol for Ecstasy?  I could have used some of that just to calm myself down.  But then so I decided to skip around and find an easier clue so I could have some letters to work with, right?  The next clue was "Flight arrives with planes flying diamonds in (6,3,5)."  What the Hell is this again?  Screw with Mort's brain day!  Were they having a sale on numerals at the NY Time.  I had to call my friend, Marion, and ask her for help.  She says to me, Mort, the answer is "apples and pears" but she had another call and hung up on me, dammit.  If the SOB at the NY Times could even remotely explain "apples and pears" then i would still need to stick him with my Ticonderoga #2 pencil.  Next clue, "Mrs. Miniver's husband in "Mrs. Miniver."  Oh, you mean Mrs. Miniver.  Thank you for the hint NY Times because i was about to confuse her with colored lady named Mrs. Miniver in Sanford and Son with Red Foxx.  Funny man, that guy was.  But I'm pretty she didn't have a husband BECAUSE SHE WAS A WIDOW NY TIMES!!  How 'bout "Four-time baseball All-Star Jose?"  Hello!  Every freaking Puerto Rican in baseball is named Jose!  "Candy since 1927."  Three letters.

11th Day Update: all 5400 slide transparencies (35mm mounted slides) have been scanned (at 2400dpi) and burned to DVDs. That was with only selective use of Color Restoration, which doesn't add anytime to the scan. We would look at each set of 4 preview images and click and highlight the ones we wanted to color restore (click on the slide, do NOT click on the checkbox...leave the checkbox on each preview CHECKED ON...that's what lets the scanner know you want to scan all 4 slides that you just previewed).

** Updated 10/29/2013 ***

The original review below remains pertinent, but here are some updates.
---
But first, reading reviews requires some thought. For example, I look at ratings to find products people are happiest with. You'd think that would be simple enough, but it is not. For example, a rating of 4.5 from 400 users may be better than a rating of 5.0 from 4 users.

Why? Imagine you think you have a great idea for a product and decide to go into business. The first thing you are likely to do is log on to Amazon and give your own product a five star review. If your smart, you'll get everyone you know to give your product a five star review.

You should read some random reviews to validate the rating, and those require "reading between the lines". Here are some common anti-patterns:

1) Poor ratings due to issues that have nothing to do with the product, such as it was broken in shipment, or there was dissatisfaction with the seller, etc. (Amazon should make it clear how to rate sellers.)

2) A highly rated product where reviews praised it for having expected attributes rather than whether it did what it claimed to do.

I could go on and on, but the general idea is to provide useful information to perspective buyers. Unfortunately this is lost on some people, so you must validate a rating by reading a s

REVIEW OF SWINGLINE OPTIMA 40 STAPLER

ALTHOUGH SWINGLINE IS A WELL-ESTABLISHED BRAND, AND GENERALLY MAKES STAPLERS OF EXCELLENT QUALITY, I GAVE THE OPTIMA 40 ONLY ONE STAR BECAUSE OF WHAT I BELIEVE TO BE TWO SERIOUS DESIGN FLAWS: 1) OVERLY COMPLICATED MECHANICALLY, AND 2) A POTENTIALLY DANGEROUS FLAT-CLINCH FEATURE THAT CAN LEAVE STAPLE ENDS EXPOSED TO PUNCTURE FINGER TIPS.

THE MECHANICAL COMPLEXITY IS OBVIOUS WHEN ONE OPENS THE STAPLER TO INSERT STAPLES. A COMPLETE DESIGN ANALYSIS IS NOT POSSIBLE HERE, BUT PLEASE NOTE THAT THE FLAT SPRING-STEEL DEVICE THAT DRIVES THE STAPLES INTO THE SHEETS OF PAPER MUST BE PERFECTLY ALIGNED TO PREVENT JAMMING, AND IT IS COMPLETELY EXPOSED WHEN RELOADING THE STAPLER. ALSO, THE FAIRLY LIGHT-WEIGHT COIL SPRING ASSEMBLY THAT SECURES THE STRIP OF STAPLES DOES NOT SEEM TO ME TO BE JAM-PROOF OR DURABLE.

BUT THE MORE SERIOUS OBJECTION IS TO THE SO-CALLED "FLAT CLINCH" FEATURE. SWINGLINE CLAIMS THAT FLAT-CLINCHING THE STAPLES PERMITS EASIER STACKING OF FINISHED PILES OF PAPER, AS OPPOSED TO THE MORE CONVENTIONAL CURVED CLINCH THAT TURNS THE STAPLE ENDS BACK INTO THE PAPER STACK.

PROBABLY TRUE, BUT UNLESS EVERY FLAT CLINCH IS PERFECTLY EXECUTED, AND LIES DEAD FLAT ON THE PAPER, AND THE STAPLED SHEETS NEVER BEND OR FLEX, THIS SMALL STACKING ADVANTAGE IS CANCELLED BY PERMITTING FLAT-CLINCHED STAPLE E
top_screaming.title: REVIEW OF SWINGLINE OPTIMA 40 STAPLER

The CD disk that came with the printer was marked with version 9.02 which I assumed was more recent than the website's Jan update (9.01). BAD ASSUMPTION, so trash the disk and use the HP website for latest software.

I discovered several new options with the printer including "school paper print", which allows one button printing blank lined paper, graph paper, music paper-great for HS kids. Also the printer allows you to connect by either USB or network (ethernet or wireless). I have both connections installed (at the same time-and it works great!)since the USB is very fast (less than 2 sec to start print), whereas the network is slow (10sec to start print), it allows others on your home router to access the printer. Also the network option allows you access the printer directly via IP address and to change more options and get status, which is quicker that the HP software.

You put the box on its side, pull out the printer, remove a few pieces of tape and that's it. Plug it in and you are pretty much ready to go. Selecting language, location and connecting to your router takes maybe one minute via the touch screen. With Mac and iOS devices you can leave it at that.

I am not sure if the CD included this, since it went straight in the trash, but I had already downloaded HP's 'HP Easy Start' software from their site and the (advanced) set up experience: checking on the firmware version, downloading a print management app, a scanning app, switching to the HP (versus Bonjour default) driver, etc., was tenfold improved over prior HP setup software.

And BTW, I'm running the 10.11 El Capitan public beta 5 OS X version, just downloaded, and everything worked just fine. I printed a double sided brochure AMD graphics card brochure with a lot of fine text and graphics as a test and the results were truly excellent.

This is a great chair but the description is a bit lacking. I almost didn't buy it because of the lack of description and reviews - which would have been a shame, because it's awesome - so I figured I'd help out (haven't been paid or reimbursed). For reference, 5'11" and 210 lbs.; your results may vary. I've had this chair for about a month now, bought it for working from home and gaming use, and have spent about 2 marathon gaming sessions in it and numerous quick sits to do some work / browse the internet.
Features: 5
* Bottom knob seems to adjust the tension in the back and lets you adjust the tilt of the seat.
* Left bottom lever unlocks / locks the recline; both the back and seat travel, though the seat is mostly stable and they travel well and naturally, so really a feature that isn't quite how I like it (I prefer the seat and back recline completely separately). Also, there's a nice bit of flex when locked so I don't feel the need to unlock and recline much.
* Forward right lever slides the seat in and out from the back, real nice feature for the price.
* Back right lever unlocks the height; lowers if you're sitting in it, raises if you're not. Standard, and not a noticeable sag which is nice, since I change the height a lot. I can set it high enough that my feet dangle (when barefoot), so tall people may actually be comfortable for once.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Extremely comfortable and adjustable chair

Allow me to digress. One of the saddest things in technology in the last decade has been the demise of HP. It's hard to imagine now, but there was a time when a HP product was like Apple products now. The HP name meant innovation, and it meant quality. Steve Jobs himself, among other tech greats, looked up to Hewlett Packard as the gold standard.

Sadly, HP printers and other products have fallen victim to the "disposable tech" trend. Most of what HP (and other tech titans) make now is crappy, plasticky junk that is built to be thrown out in a year.
